question: In JavaScript, what does `array.push(item)` return?

options.text: The new length of the array after insertion.
options.feedback: Correct: push mutates the array and returns the updated element count.

options.text: A new array containing only the pushed item.
options.feedback: Push mutates in place; creating a separate array is not its behavior.

options.text: The element that was previously at the last index.
options.feedback: Push does not expose prior tail values; it reports resulting array length.

options.text: The array itself, so methods can always be chained.
options.feedback: This confuses push with fluent APIs; push returns a number, not array.

whyQuestion: Why does `push` return length instead of the array value?

whyOptions.text: Because returning arrays would break garbage collection in V8.
whyOptions.feedback: Garbage collection is unrelated to this return contract and method semantics.

whyOptions.text: Because the operation's primary immediate result is the updated array size.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: the method signals post-mutation size, which callers often need immediately.

whyOptions.text: Because JavaScript forbids returning reference types from mutating methods.
whyOptions.feedback: JavaScript allows returning references; this is a specific API design choice.

whyOptions.text: Because `push` internally clones arrays and hides the original reference.
whyOptions.feedback: Push does not clone by default; it mutates the same array instance.

hint: It is not the modified array object.
